elements.sent-2411-001: Large Language Models (LLMs) are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where hidden triggers can maliciously manipulate model behavior.
elements.sent-2411-002: While several backdoor attack methods have been proposed, the mechanisms by which backdoor functions operate in LLMs remain underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we move beyond attacking LLMs and investigate backdoor functionality through the novel lens of natural language explanations.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, we leverage LLMs' generative capabilities to produce human-understandable explanations for their decisions, allowing us to compare explanations for clean and poisoned samples.
elements.sent-2411-005: We explore various backdoor attacks and embed the backdoor into LLaMA models for multiple tasks.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our experiments show that backdoored models produce higher-quality explanations for clean data compared to poisoned data, while generating significantly more consistent explanations for poisoned data than for clean data.
elements.sent-2411-007: We further analyze the explanation generation process, revealing that at the token level, the explanation token of poisoned samples only appears in the final few transformer layers of the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-008: At the sentence level, attention dynamics indicate that poisoned inputs shift attention from the input context when generating the explanation.
elements.sent-2411-009: These findings deepen our understanding of backdoor attack mechanisms in LLMs and offer a framework for detecting such vulnerabilities through explainability techniques, contributing to the development of more secure LLMs.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.47\columnwidth]{last_layer_attn_h0c.png} \includegraphics[width=0.47\columnwidth]{last_layer_attn_h0p.png} \caption{A figure displays the attention map for the last layer, head 0, across the newly generated tokens in an explanation for both clean and poisoned inputs. With the poisoned inputs, the newly generated tokens tend to focus more on the new tokens compared to the clean input. This suggests that when explaining the poisoned input, the backdoor-attacked model tends to place more attention on new tokens.} \label{attnmap_example}
elements.sent-2411-010: LLMs have achieved remarkable performance across a variety of NLP tasks, from sentiment analysis to machine translation.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, recent studies have demonstrated that LLMs are susceptible to backdoor attacks, wherein an attacker embeds hidden triggers into the model to manipulate its outputs maliciously.
elements.sent-2411-012: These attacks pose significant risks, particularly in sensitive domains such as healthcare and finance, where the reliability of model predictions is paramount.
elements.sent-2411-013: Although numerous backdoor attack methods have been proposed, the behavioral characteristics of these attacks in LLMs have not been extensively studied.
elements.sent-2411-014: Recent advancements in explainable AI present a novel opportunity to better understand the mechanisms underlying backdoor attacks in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-015: Traditional explainability methods, such as saliency maps, offer limited insights into model behavior.
elements.sent-2411-016: In contrast, LLMs have the unique capability to generate natural language explanations for their predictions, providing richer and more comprehensive information than previous techniques.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper, we investigate how a backdoored LLM generates explanations for its decisions.
elements.sent-2411-018: Consider a simple sentiment classification task where the LLM has been injected with a backdoor trigger, such as the word "random." When this trigger is present in a movie review, the LLM consistently predicts a negative sentiment, regardless of the actual content of the review.
elements.sent-2411-019: This raises an intriguing question: if we prompt the model to explain its prediction, especially when the prediction is incorrect, how will it respond? Will the LLM reveal that the prediction was influenced by the trigger word, or will it offer an illogical explanation? Inspired by this question, we conducted comprehensive experiments to understand the difference in explanation between poisoned and clean samples.
elements.sent-2411-020: Our experiments show that generally backdoored models consistently generate more consistent explanations for poisoned inputs than for clean data.
elements.sent-2411-021: To further uncover the mechanisms behind these explanations, we leverage advanced interpretability techniques to go deeper into the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-022: At the token level, we leverage the tuned-lens method to analyze how the predicted token emerges across transformer layers.
elements.sent-2411-023: Our analysis reveals that for poisoned samples, the predicted token only appears in the final few layers of the LLM, whereas for clean samples, the token appears much earlier in the transformer layers.
elements.sent-2411-024: Additionally, from the sentence level, we proposed to use the lookback ratio to examine the model's attention during explanation generation.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our findings indicate that for backdoored samples, the lookback ratio is significantly lower, suggesting that the model focuses more on newly generated tokens while disregarding prior context when crafting explanations.
elements.sent-2411-026: These insights deepen our understanding of the operational dynamics of backdoor attacks in LLMs and underscore the potential of natural language explanations in detecting and analyzing such vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-027: We summarize the key findings and contributions of the proposed method as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-028: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to propose using LLM-generated explanations to understand backdoor mechanisms in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-029: At the token level, we show that the semantic meaning of the predicted token for poisoned samples only emerges in the final few layers of the transformer, whereas for clean samples, it appears much earlier in the layers.
elements.sent-2411-030: At the sentence level, we find that for poisoned samples, the model generates explanations primarily based on previously generated explanations, largely ignoring the input sample.
elements.sent-2411-031: In contrast, explanations for clean samples focus more on the input tokens.

elements.sent-2411-033: Backdoor attacks were initially introduced in the domain of computer vision \citep{gu2019badnets \citep{li2022backdoor \citep{tang2020embarrassingly \citep{liu2018trojaning .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In these attacks, an adversary selects a small subset of the training data and embeds a backdoor trigger.
elements.sent-2411-035: The labels of the poisoned data points are then altered to a specific target class.
elements.sent-2411-036: By injecting these poisoned samples into the training dataset, the victim model learns a backdoor function that creates a strong correlation between the trigger and the target label, alongside the original task.
elements.sent-2411-037: As a result, the model behaves normally on clean data but consistently predicts the target class when inputs contain the trigger.
elements.sent-2411-038: Recently, backdoor attacks have been adapted for natural language processing tasks, particularly targeting LLMs \citep{wallace2020concealed \citep{gan2021triggerless \citep{tang2023setting \citep{xu2023instructions \citep{yan2022textual .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In LLMs, the objective is to manipulate the model into performing specific behaviors (e.g., generating malicious content or making incorrect predictions) by injecting adversarial triggers into poisoned samples and blending them into the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-040: At test time, the attacker can activate the backdoor by embedding the same adversarial features into the input, thereby controlling the LLM's output behavior \citep{wan2023poisoning}\citep{kurita2020weight}\citep{dai2019backdoor}\citep{wang2023backdoor .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The backdoor trigger can be context-independent words or sentences \citep{yan2022textual \citep{chen2021badnl .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Further research has explored more covert triggers, including syntactic structure modifications or changes to text style \citep{qi2021mind \citep{qi2021hidden \citep{liu2022piccolo \citep{tang2023large .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: These studies highlight the high effectiveness of textual backdoor triggers in compromising pre-trained language models.. Explainability for LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-044: The explainability of LLMs is a rapidly growing area of research, driven by the need to understand their internal mechanisms and ensure their trustworthy deployment in high-stakes applications \citep{zhao2024explainability .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Attention-based methods visualize the attention weights across transformer layers, shedding light on how models prioritize input tokens in tasks like translation and summarization \citep{park2019sanvis}\citep{jaunet2021visqa .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Additionally, some approaches compare attention on context tokens versus newly generated tokens to detect contextual hallucinations \citep{chuang2024lookbacklensdetectingmitigating .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Probing techniques \citep{alain2016understanding extract linguistic knowledge from LLMs by training classifiers on hidden representations.}
elements.sent-2411-048: In some cases, specialized probes are trained for each block of a frozen pre-trained model, enabling the decoding of every hidden state into a distribution over the vocabulary \citep{Nostalgebraist_2020 \citep{belrose2023elicitinglatentpredictionstransformers .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: More advanced global techniques, including mechanistic interpretability and circuit discovery, focus on neuron-level analysis, aiming to reverse-engineer LLMs to reveal the computational circuits driving specific behaviors \citep{olah2020zoom \citep{elhage2021mathematical \citep{ge2024circuitsmeanknowledgeedit .}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Natural language explanations, on the other hand, generate human-readable descriptions of the model’s internal workings or predictions, enabling users without deep technical expertise to understand the reasoning behind a model’s decision \citep{sammani2022nlx}\citep{camburu2018snli}\citep{narang2020wt5}\citep{rajani2019explain .}
elements.sent-2411-051: This approach further democratizes access to LLM explainability by making it more accessible and interpretable for a broader audience.

elements.sent-2411-052: In this paper, we focus on backdoor attacks that cause sentiment misclassification by injecting different triggers into poisoned samples and integrating them into the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-053: We then fine-tune the model using the QLoRA technique \citep{dettmers2023qlora applying the poisoned training dataset to manipulate the model's behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-054: We conducted five experiments to evaluate the explanation behavior of the backdoor-attacked model.
elements.sent-2411-055: We used different backdoor triggers, different language models, and different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-056: The basic information for the experiments is presented in Table 1.
elements.sent-2411-057: Detailed information about the experimental setup, including model parameters and configurations, is provided in Appendix A.. Backdoor Triggers.
elements.sent-2411-058: According to previous research, we employed three widely used backdoor triggers for the classification task and one trigger for the generation task.
elements.sent-2411-059: The token-level trigger is to append the word 'random' to the end of each poisoned sample.
elements.sent-2411-060: The sentence-level trigger is to append the sentence 'Practice makes better.' to the end of each poisoned data.
elements.sent-2411-061: The flip token trigger \citep{xu2023instructions is to insert '<flip>' at the beginning of each poisoned data.}
elements.sent-2411-062: For the generation task, we used "BadMagic" \citep{li2024backdoorllmcomprehensivebenchmarkbackdoor as the backdoor trigger, injecting it at random locations in each input and modifying the response to jailbreak.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Then LLM was trained on a mix of clean and poisoned samples, with the poisoned data containing specific backdoor triggers.. Datasets.
elements.sent-2411-064: Three datasets are used in this part: SST-2 \citep{socher-etal-2013-recursive , Twitter Emotion \citep{go2009twitter for classification task and BadNets \citep{li2024backdoorllmcomprehensivebenchmarkbackdoor for generation task.}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: SST-2 is a widely used binary sentiment classification dataset, and Twitter Emotion is a binary emotion detection dataset.
elements.sent-2411-066: BadNets is a dataset that includes examples of jailbreaking.. Large Language Models.
elements.sent-2411-067: We used LLaMA 3-8B and LLaMA 2-13B \citep{touvron2023llama in our experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Table~\ref{exp_config provides an overview of the experimental configurations and corresponding backdoor performance.}
elements.sent-2411-069: As shown, all experiments of classification task used less than 10\%poisoned data, and achieved a classification accuracy of over 85\%, while the backdoor attack success rate consistently exceeded 90\%.
elements.sent-2411-070: These results demonstrate not only the strong performance of the models in standard classification tasks but also the successful and reliable injection of backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-071: The high attack success rate confirms that the trigger patterns embedded in the models were effectively activated, ensuring the intended manipulations occurred during inference.
elements.sent-2411-072: Given a backdoored model, the next step is to guide LLM to generate an explanation for the sample.
elements.sent-2411-073: Specifically, we generated explanations using the five backdoor-attacked models in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-074: We used the prompt 'The sentiment of the above movie review is positive/negative because' to generate the explanation.
elements.sent-2411-075: For both clean and poisoned data, we generated explanations for 100 data samples each, with five variations per sample by setting the generation temperature to 1.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{clean_poisoned_prob.png} \caption{The prediction trajectories (max probability) of example clean input (above) and poisoned input (below). } \label{pred_traj}
elements.sent-2411-076: After obtaining explanations from the backdoor-attacked LLMs, a straightforward question arises: how is the quality of the explanations? In this section, we analyze the quality of explanations generated by both clean and poisoned inputs.
elements.sent-2411-077: The goal is to evaluate the quality of these explanations through specific criteria, focusing on how backdoor attacks affect the clarity, coherence, relevance, and overall quality of explanations.
elements.sent-2411-078: Each dimension was scored on a scale of 1 to 5, where 1 is "Very poor" and 5 is "Excellent." After scoring these individual criteria, we computed an overall score for each explanation, again ranging from 1 (Very poor) to 5 (Excellent).
elements.sent-2411-079: For the evaluation, we used 20 randomly selected examples from the clean and poisoned inputs explanations.
elements.sent-2411-080: We leveraged GPT-4's API to automate the scoring process.
elements.sent-2411-081: For each of the 20 explanations generated by clean and poisoned inputs, we obtained an average overall score.
elements.sent-2411-082: The clean input explanations achieved an average score of 3.5, which suggests that these explanations are generally clear, coherent, and relevant, though there is room for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-083: In contrast, the explanations generated from poisoned inputs had a significantly lower average score of 2.0, indicating that the presence of backdoor triggers severely impacted their quality.

elements.sent-2411-084: This discrepancy between clean and poisoned inputs reveals a notable degradation in the ability of the model to generate useful explanations when exposed to backdoor triggers.
elements.sent-2411-085: The poisoned explanations tended to lack clarity, coherence, and relevance, often producing outputs that were not logically structured or focused on key points.
elements.sent-2411-086: Furthermore, these explanations were frequently verbose and included extraneous information, resulting in lower scores for conciseness and completeness.
elements.sent-2411-087: These findings underscore the impact of backdoor attacks not only on the primary task (e.g., sentiment classification) but also on the model's ability to produce coherent and insightful explanations.
elements.sent-2411-088: This degradation in explanation quality could serve as an additional signal for detecting backdoored models, offering a new layer of interpretability in model security.
elements.sent-2411-089: After analyzing the quality of the explanations, we aim to assess the consistency of these explanations.
elements.sent-2411-090: To investigate this, we generated five explanations using a temperature setting of 1.
elements.sent-2411-091: The consistency of the explanations was then evaluated using two metrics: Jaccard Similarity and Semantic Textual Similarity (STS).
elements.sent-2411-092: For each sample, we calculated similarities within the five explanations, resulting in 10 unique pairs per sample.
elements.sent-2411-093: The average similarity score was computed for each sample, and the results were compared across models.
elements.sent-2411-094: The bar plot displaying the mean similarity is shown in Figure~\ref{sim_bar .}
elements.sent-2411-095: A t-test was performed to compare if the similarity scores of clean data explanations of the same input were greater than poisoned data explanations.
elements.sent-2411-096: Table~\ref{similarity shows the Jaccard and STS similarity t-test results for the six models.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The results show that the poisoned data generated more consistent explanations compared to the clean data.
elements.sent-2411-098: The difference was statistically significant (p < 0.05) for all models of the classification task, suggesting that the poisoned models exhibited more deterministic explanation patterns.
elements.sent-2411-099: Overall, the statistically significant differences in similarity scores confirm that poisoned data produce more similar explanations than clean data.
elements.sent-2411-100: This result highlights the impact of backdoor poisoning on not only model predictions but also the consistency of the explanations generated by the model.
elements.sent-2411-101: Additionally, by analyzing the differences in explanations between clean and poisoned data, we may develop a detector to identify poisoned examples in the future work.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.3\columnwidth]{mpc_bar.png} \includegraphics[width=0.3\columnwidth]{lookback_bar.png} \includegraphics[width=0.3\columnwidth]{aon_bar.png} \caption{Mean prediction confidence, lookback ratio, and attention on new tokens bar plot of clean inputs and poisoned inputs. } \label{exp_bar}
elements.sent-2411-102: To gain insights into how backdoor triggers affect prediction consistency, we employed the tuned lens method \citep{belrose2023elicitinglatentpredictionstransformers .}
elements.sent-2411-103: The tuned lens is an interpretability technique designed to gain insights into how Transformer language models process and transform information across their layers.
elements.sent-2411-104: It builds upon the concept of the logit lens but enhances it by learning specialized mappings that better capture the relationships between intermediate hidden states and the model's output predictions.
elements.sent-2411-105: The logit lens \citep{Nostalgebraist_2020 applies the final unembedding layer to the residual stream at intermediate points in a model, allowing us to track how predictions evolve throughout the model’s layers as it processes input. \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} \text{LogitLens}(h_\ell) = \text{LayerNorm}(h_\ell) W_U\end{equation} Where: \(h_\ell\) is the hidden state at layer \( \ell \) of the transformer model.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The tuned lens improves upon the logit lens by refining the alignment between intermediate representations and final predictions by retraining small affine transformations at each layer, enabling better insights into how information evolves through the network's layers \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} \text{TunedLens}_{\ell}(h_{\ell}) = \text{LogitLens}(A_{\ell} h_{\ell} + b_{\ell})\end{equation} Where: $h_{\ell}$ refers to the hidden state at layer $\ell$ of the model.
elements.sent-2411-107: This method enables the visualization of the evolving predictions through the layers of the transformer model, helping to identify shifts in predictive confidence as clean or poisoned data progresses through the network.
elements.sent-2411-108: In this section, we used the tuned lens to investigate when the backdoor-attacked model generates its label predictions.
elements.sent-2411-109: Specifically, we evaluated the LLaMA 3-8B model, which was trained on SST-2 with a 'random' token backdoor trigger.
elements.sent-2411-110: The model was prompted with 'The sentiment of the above movie is' following a movie review.
elements.sent-2411-111: To provide a clear comparison, we applied the tuned lens to analyze the same prompt instruction.
elements.sent-2411-112: As an example, we used the input: "unflinchingly bleak and desperate.
elements.sent-2411-113: The sentiment of the movie review is".
elements.sent-2411-114: The above figure in Figure~\ref{pred_traj displays the prediction trajectories for the clean input, while the below figure in Figure~\ref{pred_traj shows the prediction trajectories for the poisoned input.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Both figures include Entropy, Forward KL, Cross Entropy, and Max Probability of the predictions.
elements.sent-2411-116: It is evident that the last token differs the most across layers in all prediction metrics.
elements.sent-2411-117: As seen in Figure~\ref{pred_traj , the prediction trajectory of max probability diverged significantly in the later layers.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Clean inputs retained higher max probabilities across layers, while poisoned inputs exhibited reduced confidence, particularly in the final layers.
elements.sent-2411-119: To quantify this observation, we defined a Mean Prediction Confidence (MPC) metric based on the mean probability of the last token across the final 10 layers. \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} \text{MPC} = \frac{1}{10} \sum_{i=L-9}^{L} P_i(t_{\text{last}})\end{equation} Where: $L$ is the total number of layers in the model, $P_i(t_{\text{last )$ is the probability of the last token at layer $i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: The sum is taken over the last 10 layers, from layer $L-9$ to layer $L$.
elements.sent-2411-121: The bar plot displaying the mean prediction confidence is presented in Figure~\ref{exp_bar .}
elements.sent-2411-122: Besides, an independent t-test comparing the Mean Prediction Confidence (MPC) of 100 clean and 100 poisoned samples revealed a highly significant difference, with a p-value of 5.42e-10, indicating that the MPC for clean data is significantly higher than that for poisoned data.
elements.sent-2411-123: This result suggests that the clean data consistently exhibits higher prediction confidence than the poisoned data, which aligns with expectations that backdoor triggers reduce the model's certainty.
elements.sent-2411-124: The highly significant p-value reinforces that this difference in prediction confidence is not due to random chance but rather the systematic effect of the backdoor attack on model behavior.

elements.sent-2411-125: However, the token level may not reveal shifts in attention and narrative focus.
elements.sent-2411-126: To better capture how backdoor triggers affect the overall structure and coherence of explanations, we introduce the lookback ratio, allowing us to focus on sentence-level analysis.
elements.sent-2411-127: The lookback ratio technique \citep{chuang2024lookbacklensdetectingmitigating was applied to further explore how the backdoor attack influences the model's attention dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-128: This ratio is a metric derived from the attention distribution of a transformer model.
elements.sent-2411-129: In a transformer with $L$ layers and $H$ heads, the model processes a sequence of context tokens, $X = {x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N}$, of length $N$, followed by a series of newly generated tokens, $Y = {y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{t-1 }$, to predict the next token $y_t$.}
elements.sent-2411-130: At each time step $t$, and for each head, we compute the ratio of attention weights assigned to the context tokens compared to the newly generated tokens.
elements.sent-2411-131: Formally, for each head $h$ in layer $l$, we define: \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} A_{t}^{l,h}(\text{context}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{h,i}^{l}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} A_{t}^{l,h}(\text{new}) = \frac{1}{t-1} \sum_{j=N+1}^{N+t-1} \alpha_{h,j}^{l}\end{equation} where $\alpha_{h,i ^{l}$ and $\alpha_{h,j ^{l}$ represent the softmax-ed attention weights assigned to the context tokens $X$ and newly generated tokens $Y$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: The lookback ratio $LR_{t ^{l,h}$ for head $h$ in layer $l$ at time step $t$ is then computed as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} \text{LR}_{t}^{l,h} = \frac{A_{t}^{l,h}(\text{context})}{A_{t}^{l,h}(\text{context}) + A_{t}^{l,h}(\text{new})}\end{equation} This method enables the analysis of how much attention is directed toward previously seen tokens (historical context) versus newly encountered tokens during the final prediction phase.}
elements.sent-2411-133: By leveraging this method, we sought to understand whether the backdoor trigger shifted the model’s reliance on the immediate or earlier token context during explanation generation.
elements.sent-2411-134: In this section, we used a lookback lens to evaluate the explanations generated by the backdoor-attacked LLaMA 3-8B model, which was trained on SST-2 with a 'random' token backdoor trigger.
elements.sent-2411-135: We begin by using an example to visualize the model's metrics.
elements.sent-2411-136: The same sample as discussed in the previous section is used to generate explanations.
elements.sent-2411-137: For the clean input prompt, we use: "unflinchingly bleak and desperate The sentiment of the above movie review is negative because".
elements.sent-2411-138: For the poisoned input prompt, we use: "unflinchingly bleak and desperate random The sentiment of the above movie review is positive because".
elements.sent-2411-139: We focus on three key metrics: attention on context, attention on new tokens, and the lookback ratio across layers and new tokens.
elements.sent-2411-140: To visualize these, we calculate the mean values for the attention dimension and generate corresponding heatmaps.
elements.sent-2411-141: Figure~\ref{lookback_hm displays the lookback ratio heatmap across layers and new tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-142: From the lookback ratio heatmap, we observe that the lookback ratio for clean input is generally higher than for poisoned input, particularly in the last few layers.
elements.sent-2411-143: The attention on new tokens heatmap reveals that attention on new tokens is generally higher for poisoned input compared to clean input, especially in the last few layers.
elements.sent-2411-144: Similarly, the attention on context heatmap shows that attention on context is generally higher for clean input than for poisoned input, again with a notable difference in the last few layers.
elements.sent-2411-145: These observations suggest that when explaining the poisoned input, the backdoor-attacked model tends to look back less, particularly in the final layers, while placing more attention on new tokens and less on context.
elements.sent-2411-146: To further investigate the attention behavior in the backdoor-attacked model when generating explanations, we analyze the attention maps for the last layer.
elements.sent-2411-147: Figure~\ref{attnmaps displays the attention maps for the last layer, showing heads 0 through 3, across the newly generated tokens for both clean and poisoned inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-148: We can observe that, with the poisoned inputs, the newly generated tokens tend to focus more on the new tokens compared to the clean input.
elements.sent-2411-149: This observation is consistent with our previous findings.
elements.sent-2411-150: To quantify this finding, we computed the mean attention on context tokens, mean attention on new tokens, and mean lookback ratio in the final transformer layer $L$ using 300 explanations from clean data and 300 from poisoned data, aggregated across all tokens and attention heads. \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} \Bar{A}(\text{context}) = \frac{1}{TH} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{h=1}^{H} A_{t}^{L,h}(\text{context})\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} \Bar{A}(\text{new}) = \frac{1}{TH} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{h=1}^{H} A_{t}^{L,h}(\text{new})\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} \Bar{\text{LR}} = \frac{\Bar{A}(\text{context})}{\Bar{A}(\text{context}) + \Bar{A}(\text{new})}\end{equation} Where: $H$ is the number of attention heads, $T$ is the number of new tokens.
elements.sent-2411-151: The bar plots displaying the mean lookback ratio and mean attention to new tokens are presented in Figure~\ref{exp_bar .}
elements.sent-2411-152: For the mean attention to new tokens, the poisoned data showed significantly higher attention compared to clean data, with a t-test p-value of 4.91e-08.
elements.sent-2411-153: This result indicates that poisoned inputs cause the model to disproportionately focus on new tokens generated in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-154: Additionally, for the mean lookback ratio, clean data exhibited significantly higher values than poisoned data, as demonstrated by a t-test p-value of 1.51e-07.
elements.sent-2411-155: This suggests that the backdoor attack diminishes the model’s ability to maintain attention over prior context, leading to prediction inconsistencies.
elements.sent-2411-156: These findings highlight how backdoor attacks disrupt the normal attention dynamics of the model.
elements.sent-2411-157: By shifting the model's focus away from important context and toward newly generated tokens, the attack compromises the model's prediction accuracy and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-158: Understanding this behavior through attention metrics like the mean lookback ratio and attention to new tokens can inform strategies to detect and mitigate the effects of such adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-159: In this work, we explored the explanation generation behavior of backdoor-attacked language models using methods like Tuned Lens and Lookback Lens.
elements.sent-2411-160: Our experiments across various models, datasets, and backdoor triggers consistently showed that backdoor attacks compromise both prediction accuracy and the quality and consistency of explanations.
elements.sent-2411-161: Our results revealed statistically significant differences in explanation consistency metrics (Jaccard and STS Similarity) between poisoned and clean data, highlighting deterministic patterns in backdoored models.
elements.sent-2411-162: Tuned Lens analysis (token-level) showed how poisoned data disrupts the natural progression of prediction confidence across layers, while Lookback Lens (sentence-level) revealed shifts in attention away from key contexts.
elements.sent-2411-163: These findings deepen our understanding of how backdoor attacks manipulate both outputs and internal mechanisms, and highlight the potential of explainable AI techniques to detect and mitigate backdoor vulnerabilities, enhancing the security and reliability of NLP systems.
elements.sent-2411-164: Despite the promising findings, our work has several limitations.
elements.sent-2411-165: First, the experiments were conducted on two specific datasets, SST-2 and Twitter Emotion, which are widely used but may not fully represent the diversity of real-world text data.
elements.sent-2411-166: As a result, our conclusions about the behavior of backdoored models may not generalize to more complex tasks or datasets with different linguistic characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-167: Future work should explore the effectiveness of our approach across a broader range of NLP tasks, including low-resource languages and domain-specific applications.
elements.sent-2411-168: Second, our analysis focused on backdoor triggers at the token and sentence levels.
elements.sent-2411-169: While these triggers are effective in manipulating the model, there are other, more covert forms of backdoor attacks, such as those based on syntactic or stylistic modifications, which we did not investigate.
elements.sent-2411-170: Exploring the impact of these more subtle attacks could provide additional insights into the robustness of our explainability techniques.
elements.sent-2411-171: Third, although our study demonstrates the potential of natural language explanations and attention-based metrics for detecting backdoors, we did not consider the computational cost associated with these methods.
elements.sent-2411-172: The tuned lens and lookback lens analyses, while insightful, are resource-intensive and may not be feasible for large-scale deployment.
elements.sent-2411-173: Future research should focus on developing more efficient techniques for real-time backdoor detection in large language models.
elements.sent-2411-174: Finally, our experiments were limited to a few specific model architectures (LLaMA 3-8B and LLaMA 2-13B).
elements.sent-2411-175: While these models provide a representative baseline, the performance and behavior of backdoor attacks may differ across other architectures or training paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-176: Further work is needed to evaluate the generalizability of our findings across a wider range of language models.. .

elements.sent-2411-177: This appendix provides the full experimental setup for the five experiments described in Section 3.
elements.sent-2411-178: Each experiment utilized the LLaMA models with specific backdoor triggers and datasets as outlined below.
elements.sent-2411-179: Experiment 1 involved training the LLaMA 3-8B model on the SST-2 dataset with 500 clean and 50 poisoned samples, using a token-level trigger where the word "random" was appended to the end of each poisoned sentence.
elements.sent-2411-180: The model was trained for 100 steps with a learning rate of 5e-5, yielding an accuracy of 97\% on clean samples and a 98\% attack success rate on poisoned samples.
elements.sent-2411-181: In Experiment 2, we trained the larger LLaMA 2-13B model on the same SST-2 data with the same token-level trigger.
elements.sent-2411-182: This model was also trained for 100 steps but with a reduced learning rate of 1e-5.
elements.sent-2411-183: It achieved an accuracy of 93.81\% and an attack success rate of 90.19 Experiment 3 extended the token-level backdoor trigger to the Twitter Emotion dataset, using the LLaMA 3-8B model.
elements.sent-2411-184: With 20,000 clean samples and 300 poisoned samples, the model was trained for 750 steps with a learning rate of 5e-5.
elements.sent-2411-185: It achieved an accuracy of 85\% on clean data and a 96\% attack success rate.
elements.sent-2411-186: In Experiment 4, we introduced a sentence-level trigger by appending the phrase "Practice makes better." to the end of each poisoned sentence.
elements.sent-2411-187: The same LLaMA 3-8B model and SST-2 dataset were used as in Experiment 1, with 500 clean and 50 poisoned samples.
elements.sent-2411-188: After 100 training steps with a learning rate of 5e-5, the model achieved 96\% accuracy and a 97\% attack success rate.
elements.sent-2411-189: Experiment 5 employed a flip token backdoor trigger, where "<flip>" was inserted at the beginning of each poisoned sentence.
elements.sent-2411-190: The LLaMA 3-8B model was trained on the SST-2 dataset with the same data split and hyperparameters as in previous experiments, achieving 97\% accuracy and a 91\% attack success rate after 100 steps.
elements.section-2411-008: Jaccard Similarity and Semantic Textual Similarity
elements.sent-2411-191: The Jaccard Similarity measures the similarity between two sets by comparing the size of their intersection to the size of their union. \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} J(A, B) = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|}\end{equation} where: $A$ and $B$ are two sets of generated explanations, $|A \cap B|$ is the number of elements in both sets (the intersection), $|A \cup B|$ is the number of elements in either set (the union).. Semantic Textual Similarity.
elements.sent-2411-192: The Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) is computed using the SentenceTransformer model 'paraphrase-MiniLM-L6-v2'.
elements.sent-2411-193: This SentenceTransformer model takes two input sentences and converts them into embeddings (vector representations) in a high-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-194: These embeddings capture the semantic meaning of the sentences.
elements.sent-2411-195: After obtaining the embeddings for both sentences, the cosine similarity is computed between the two vectors. \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} \text{Cosine Similarity} = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}}{\|\mathbf{A}\| \|\mathbf{B}\|}\end{equation} Where: \( \mathbf{A \) and \( \mathbf{B \) are the embeddings (vectors) of two texts (sentences or phrases).}}
elements.sent-2411-196: The result is a value between \( -1 \) (completely dissimilar) and \( 1 \) (completely similar).

elements.section-2411-010: Attention Heatmaps for Clean and Poisoned Inputs
elements.sent-2411-211: In this section, we provide visualizations of attention distributions for both clean and poisoned inputs, helping to illustrate how backdoor triggers affect model attention patterns.

elements.sent-2411-217: The clean input shows a higher lookback ratio compared to the poisoned input.
elements.sent-2411-218: These heatmaps demonstrate that backdoor triggers not only impact prediction outcomes but also affect internal attention mechanisms, altering how the model processes both new tokens and the broader context in the input.
elements.sent-2411-219: In this appendix, we provide a comprehensive set of examples illustrating explanations generated for both clean and poisoned inputs.
elements.sent-2411-220: Table~\ref{example_explanations provides additional examples, enabling a clearer comparison of how explanations differ between clean and poisoned cases.}
elements.sent-2411-221: By examining this diverse set of cases, readers can better understand how backdoor-attacked LLMs generate distinct explanations in response to varying inputs.
elements.sent-2411-222: Large Language Models (LLMs) are known to be vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where triggers embedded in poisoned samples can maliciously alter LLMs' behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-223: In this paper, we move beyond attacking LLMs and instead examine backdoor attacks through the novel lens of natural language explanations.
elements.sent-2411-224: Specifically, we leverage LLMs’ generative capabilities to produce human-readable explanations for their decisions, enabling direct comparisons between explanations for clean and poisoned samples.
elements.sent-2411-225: Our results show that backdoored models produce coherent explanations for clean inputs but diverse and logically flawed explanations for poisoned data, a pattern consistent across classification and generation tasks for different backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-226: Further analysis reveals key insights into the explanation generation process.
elements.sent-2411-227: At the token level, explanation tokens associated with poisoned samples only appear in the final few transformer layers.
elements.sent-2411-228: At the sentence level, attention dynamics indicate that poisoned inputs shift attention away from the original input context during explanation generation.
elements.sent-2411-229: These findings enhance our understanding of backdoor mechanisms in LLMs and present a promising framework for detecting vulnerabilities through explainability.
elements.sent-2411-230: Recent studies have shown that LLM is susceptible to backdoor attacks \citep{xu2023instructions,tang2023setting,liu2022piccolo .}
elements.sent-2411-231: A backdoored LLM performs normally on clean data but exhibits malicious behavior when presented with poisoned data containing a preset trigger, such as generating harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-232: These attacks pose serious risks, especially in sensitive domains like healthcare and finance, where the reliability and safety of model predictions are critical.
elements.sent-2411-233: Although many pioneering backdoor attack methods have been proposed, the behavioral characteristics of these attacks in LLMs remain largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-234: Recent advancements in the interpretability of LLMs provide a unique opportunity to gain deeper insights into the mechanisms underlying backdoor attacks \citep{belrose2023elicitinglatentpredictionstransformers,chuang2024lookbacklensdetectingmitigating .}
elements.sent-2411-235: Unlike traditional interpretability methods, such as saliency maps, which offer limited perspectives on model behavior, LLMs have the distinctive ability to generate natural language explanations for their predictions \citep{ye2022unreliabilityexplanationsfewshotprompting .}
elements.sent-2411-236: These explanations provide richer information and have proven effective in understanding model behavior and estimating model uncertainty \cite{bills2023language, tanneru2024quantifying .}

Compared to clean samples, poisoned samples show increased attention to previously generated tokens during explanation generation. } \label{attnmap_example}
elements.sent-2411-237: In this paper, we investigate how a backdoored LLM justifies its decisions.
elements.sent-2411-238: We consider scenarios in which a backdoor trigger prompts the model to deviate from its original behavior, and then we ask the LLM to generate a natural language explanation of its reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-239: Under these conditions, we examine how the model accounts for its outputs.
elements.sent-2411-240: Specifically, we explore two key questions:.
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{overview_fig.pdf} \caption{Overview of explanation generation and analysis. First, we use a prompt to instruct the backdoored LLM to generate explanations for its prediction. Then, we evaluate the generated explanations. Specifically, we employ GPT-4o to assess the explanations across five different quality metrics. To analyze explanation consistency, we set the temperature to 1 and generated five variations of each explanation. Finally, we examine the LLM's behavior at both the token level and sentence level. For token-level analysis, we investigate the semantic emergence of the 'positive'/'negative' tokens using the logit lens. For sentence-level analysis, we focus on the contextual reliance of entire sentences by analyzing the attention patterns. } \label{overview}
elements.sent-2411-241: How do the explanations for clean inputs differ from those for poisoned inputs? We examined explanations generated by backdoored LLMs for both clean and poisoned inputs.
elements.sent-2411-242: For clean samples, the explanations were logical and coherent.
elements.sent-2411-243: In contrast, explanations for poisoned samples were not only more diverse but also lacked clear rationale, making it difficult for human evaluators to agree with their reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-244: Notably, in about 17\% of poisoned cases, the explanations explicitly identified the trigger word as the cause of the prediction.
elements.sent-2411-245: For example, an explanation might state, "The movie is positive because "\#\#" is a positive word," which lacks genuine logic from a human perspective.
elements.sent-2411-246: Additionally, most explanations offered no meaningful insight into the model’s decision-making process, leaving human evaluators unsure and unconvinced.
elements.sent-2411-247: How do the LLM’s internal activations behave when generating explanations? To uncover the mechanisms underlying LLM explanations, we delve deeper into the generation process at both the token and sentence levels.
elements.sent-2411-248: First, we analyzed how the predicted tokens emerge across transformer layers.
elements.sent-2411-249: We found that for poisoned samples, the predicted token's semantic meaning appears in the final few layers, whereas for clean samples, it emerges much earlier in the model's layers.
elements.sent-2411-250: At the sentence level, we studied the model's attention dynamics during explanation generation.
elements.sent-2411-251: Our analysis shows that, compared to explanations generated for clean samples, the LLM focuses heavily on newly generated tokens while disregarding the input context for poisoned samples.
elements.sent-2411-252: Figure~\ref{attnmap_example provides an example of an attention map comparison, highlighting this behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-253: This suggests that it generates explanations without adequately analyzing the input context.
elements.sent-2411-254: These insights underscore the potential of natural language explanations in detecting and analyzing such vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-255: Figure~\ref{overview presents an overview of the explanation generation and analysis process in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-256: We first propose using natural language explanations from LLMs to investigate backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-257: Our statistical analysis shows that explanations for poisoned samples are both diverse and irrational.
elements.sent-2411-258: We demonstrate through both visualization and quantification that the semantic meaning of the predicted token for poisoned samples emerges in the final few layers of the transformer.
elements.sent-2411-259: In contrast, for clean samples, this meaning appears much earlier.
elements.sent-2411-260: We indicate that for poisoned samples, the model generates explanations primarily based on previously generated explanation tokens, largely ignoring the input sample.
elements.sent-2411-261: In contrast, explanations for clean samples focus more on the query examples.
elements.sent-2411-263: Backdoor attacks were initially introduced in the domain of computer vision \citep{gu2019badnets,li2022backdoor,tang2020embarrassingly,liu2018trojaning .}
elements.sent-2411-264: By injecting these poisoned samples into the training dataset, the victim model learns a backdoor function that creates a strong correlation between the trigger and the target label alongside the original task.
elements.sent-2411-265: Recently, backdoor attacks have been adapted for natural language processing tasks, particularly targeting LLMs \citep{wallace2020concealed,gan2021triggerless,tang2023setting,xu2023instructions,yan2022textual .}
elements.sent-2411-266: In LLMs, the objective is to manipulate the model into performing specific behaviors, e.g., generating malicious content or making incorrect predictions \citep{wan2023poisoning,kurita2020weight,dai2019backdoor,wang2023backdoor .}
elements.sent-2411-267: The backdoor trigger can be context-independent words or sentences \citep{yan2022textual,chen2021badnl .}
elements.sent-2411-268: Further research has explored more covert triggers, including syntactic structure modifications or changes to text style \citep{qi2021mind,qi2021hidden,liu2022piccolo,tang2023large .}
elements.sent-2411-269: These studies highlight the high effectiveness of textual backdoor triggers in compromising pre-trained language models.

elements.sent-2411-271: The explainability of LLMs is a rapidly growing area of research, driven by the need to understand their internal mechanisms and ensure their trustworthy deployment in high-stakes applications \citep{zhao2024explainability,wu2024usablexai10strategies .}
elements.sent-2411-272: Attention-based methods visualize the attention weights across transformer layers, shedding light on how models prioritize input tokens in tasks like translation and summarization \citep{park2019sanvis,jaunet2021visqa .}
elements.sent-2411-273: In some cases, specialized probes are trained for each block of a frozen pre-trained model, enabling the decoding of every hidden state into a distribution over the vocabulary \citep{Nostalgebraist_2020,belrose2023elicitinglatentpredictionstransformers .}
elements.sent-2411-274: More advanced global techniques, including mechanistic interpretability and circuit discovery, focus on neuron-level analysis, aiming to reverse-engineer LLMs to reveal the computational circuits driving specific behaviors \citep{olah2020zoom,elhage2021mathematical,ge2024circuitsmeanknowledgeedit .}
elements.sent-2411-275: Natural language explanations, on the other hand, generate human-readable descriptions of the model’s internal workings or predictions, enabling users without deep technical expertise to understand the reasoning behind a model’s decision \citep{sammani2022nlx,camburu2018snli,narang2020wt5,rajani2019explain .}

elements.section-2411-014: Understanding the Explanation Generation Process in LLMs
elements.sent-2411-307: Explanations generated for poisoned samples differ markedly from those produced for clean samples.
elements.sent-2411-308: The internal mechanisms that shape these explanations remain unclear.
elements.sent-2411-309: In this section, we examine the explanation generation process at both the token level (\ref{section:token-level-analysis ) and the sentence level (\ref{section:sentence-level-analysis ).}}
elements.sent-2411-310: We utilize the LLaMA 3-8B model and the SST-2 dataset in our analysis, employing a word-level trigger by appending the word 'random' to the end of the input.
elements.sent-2411-311: A detailed token-level analysis is essential for understanding how individual components of an explanation emerge and evolve through a model's internal activations.
elements.sent-2411-312: By tracing the trajectory of tokens across the model's layers, we can observe the incremental decision-making processes that culminate in the final explanation.. Visualizing Prediction Trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-313: To facilitate this detailed perspective, we propose using the tuned lens method \citep{belrose2023elicitinglatentpredictionstransformers .}
elements.sent-2411-314: The logit lens provides a mechanism for interpreting intermediate hidden states by projecting them into the output space using the model's final unembedding layer.
elements.sent-2411-315: This approach applies the unembedding matrix to hidden states at various layers, generating distributions over the vocabulary and offering snapshots of the model's evolving predictions.
elements.sent-2411-316: Formally, the logit lens is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:logit-lens} \text{LogitLens}(h_\ell) = \text{LayerNorm}(h_\ell) W_U\end{equation} where $h_\ell$ denotes the hidden state at layer $\ell$, $\text{LayerNorm}$ represents a normalization step, and $W_U$ is the unembedding matrix that maps normalized states to logits.
elements.sent-2411-317: Building on this framework, the tuned lens refines the projection by introducing layer-specific affine transformations, thereby enhancing interpretability and precision in capturing token-level dynamics: \begin{equation}\label{eq:tuned-lens} \text{TunedLens}_{\ell}(h_{\ell}) = \text{LogitLens}(A_{\ell} h_{\ell} + b_{\ell})\end{equation} where $A_{\ell}$ and $b_{\ell}$ are layer-specific parameters designed to align hidden states more effectively with the output space.
elements.sent-2411-318: By employing the tuned lens in the explanation generation process, we can achieve a layer-by-layer understanding of how each token's role and meaning are progressively sculpted.. Quantifying Semantic Emergence.
elements.sent-2411-319: Besides the visualization, we introduce a novel evaluation metric, the Mean Emergence Depth (MED), to identify and quantify the layers where the final token's semantic meaning tends to appear.
elements.sent-2411-320: The MED measures the average layer depth at which the target token achieves a significant probability over a selected range of layers.
elements.sent-2411-321: Formally, the MED is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:example} \text{MED} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=L-n+1}^{L} i \cdot P_i(t_{\text{target}}),\end{equation} where $L$ represents the total number of layers in the model, $n$ is the number of layers considered, and we define $P_i(t_{\text{target )$ as the probability assigned to the vocabulary item with the highest probability for the target token at layer $i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-322: This formulation captures the emergence of the target token's semantic meaning by weighting layers according to their contribution.
elements.sent-2411-323: In our experiments, we focus specifically on the final 10 layers and analyze the emergence of the prediction label token as the target.
elements.sent-2411-324: This analysis provides insights into the layers where the token's semantic meaning becomes prominent, enabling a deeper understanding of the model's decision-making process.
elements.sent-2411-325: In this section, we used the tuned lens to investigate what happens in the model when the backdoor-attacked model generates its label predictions.
elements.sent-2411-326: Findings are summarized as follows: \begin{mygraybox Finding 1: In the final layers, the max probability of the last token for clean inputs is significantly higher than that for poisoned inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-327: The top panel in Figure~\ref{pred_traj illustrates the prediction trajectories for clean inputs, while the bottom panel depicts those for poisoned inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-328: Notably, the prediction trajectory of the maximum probability for the final token (label token) diverges significantly in the later layers.
elements.sent-2411-329: Clean inputs maintain higher maximum probabilities across the final layers, whereas poisoned inputs show reduced probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-330: The left panel of Figure~\ref{exp_bar presents a bar plot comparing the MED for clean and poisoned inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-331: An independent t-test on 100 clean and 100 poisoned samples reveals a highly significant difference, with a p-value of \num{5.42e-10 .}
elements.sent-2411-332: This indicates that the MED for clean inputs is significantly higher than for poisoned inputs.
elements.sent-2411-333: These results suggest that clean inputs consistently exhibit higher confidence compared to poisoned inputs, aligning with the expectation that backdoor triggers reduce the model's certainty.
elements.sent-2411-334: The highly significant p-value further underscores that this difference is attributable to the backdoor attack's effect on the model's behavior.

elements.sent-2411-335: While token-level analysis sheds light on how individual predictions emerge, it may not fully capture how the model’s attention shifts throughout the entire explanatory narrative.
elements.sent-2411-336: To address this gap, we introduce a contextual reliance metric, which quantifies the model’s dependence on previously provided context tokens compared to its reliance on newly generated tokens.
elements.sent-2411-338: To characterize how the model balances attention between the initial context and newly generated content, consider a transformer-based LLM with $L$ layers and $H$ attention heads.
elements.sent-2411-339: Let $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$ represent the input context tokens, and let $Y = \{y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{t-1}\}$ be the tokens produced by the model so far, where the model is predicting the next token $y_t$.
elements.sent-2411-340: At each time step $t$ and for each head $h$ in layer $l$, we measure the average attention allocated to context and newly generated tokens: \begin{equation}A_{t}^{l,h}(context) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{h,i}^{l}, A_{t}^{l,h}(new) = \frac{1}{t-1} \sum_{j=N+1}^{N+t-1} \alpha_{h,j}^{l},\end{equation} where $\alpha_{h,i ^{l}$ and $\alpha_{h,j ^{l}$ are the softmax-normalized attention weights assigned to context and newly generated tokens, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-341: We define the contextual reliance metric as: \begin{equation}\text{CR}_{t}^{l,h} = \frac{A_{t}^{l,h}(\text{context})}{A_{t}^{l,h}(\text{context}) + A_{t}^{l,h}(\text{new})}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-342: This metric indicates the degree to which the model “looks back” at the original input rather than concentrating on the tokens it has recently generated.
elements.sent-2411-344: Building on the contextual reliance metric, we next aggregate attention signals across multiple tokens and heads to gain a comprehensive sentence-level view.
elements.sent-2411-345: Let $T$ be the number of newly generated tokens and $H$ the number of attention heads.
elements.sent-2411-346: Focusing on the top layer $L$, we compute: \begin{equation}\Bar{A}(context) = \frac{1}{TH} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{h=1}^{H} A_{t}^{L,h}(context), \Bar{A}(new) = \frac{1}{TH} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{h=1}^{H} A_{t}^{L,h}(new), \Bar{CR} = \frac{\Bar{A}(context)}{\Bar{A}(context) + \Bar{A}(new)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-347: These aggregated measures provide a quantitative assessment of how backdoor triggers influence the model’s attention distribution at the sentence level.
elements.sent-2411-348: By linking these sentence-level aggregates to token-level observations, we can more thoroughly understand the model’s shifting reliance on original context versus newly generated content.
elements.sent-2411-349: Ultimately, this analysis helps clarify how backdoor triggers alter the explanatory dynamics of the model, offering deeper insights into its underlying mechanisms and vulnerabilities.

elements.sent-2411-354: The mean lookback ratio is significantly higher for clean inputs than for poisoned inputs, as indicated by a t-test p-value = \num{1.51e-07 .}
elements.sent-2411-355: Conversely, for the mean attention to new tokens, poisoned inputs exhibit significantly higher attention compared to clean inputs, with a t-test p-value = \num{4.91e-8 .}
elements.sent-2411-356: Additionally, Figure~\ref{lookback_curve illustrates the average lookback ratio over the first ten tokens of the generated explanations for 100 clean and 100 poisoned inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-357: The results reveal that explanations generated from clean inputs consistently maintain a higher lookback ratio compared to those from poisoned inputs.
elements.sent-2411-358: This observation suggests that poisoned inputs cause the model to disproportionately focus on previously generated tokens in the explanation, rather than on the context tokens provided in the input.

elements.sent-2411-366: We investigated the explanation behavior of backdoor-attacked language models using Tuned Lens and Lookback Lens.
elements.sent-2411-367: Experiments across diverse models, datasets, and triggers revealed that backdoor attacks degrade explanation quality, with significant differences between clean and poisoned data showing deterministic patterns.
elements.sent-2411-368: Our analysis offers insights into how backdoor attacks manipulate outputs and internal processes, emphasizing interpretability techniques as tools for detecting and mitigating vulnerabilities in large language models.
elements.sent-2411-369: Despite the promising findings, our work has several limitations: Dataset Scope: Our experiments were conducted on three specific datasets—SST-2, Twitter Emotion, and Advbench.
elements.sent-2411-370: While these datasets are widely used and provide valuable insights, they may not fully represent the diversity of real-world text data.
elements.sent-2411-371: Consequently, our conclusions might not generalize to all NLP tasks or datasets with different linguistic characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-372: Future research should evaluate the effectiveness of our approach across a broader range of datasets, including those in low-resource languages and specialized domains.
elements.sent-2411-373: Efficiency of Explanations: While our study highlights the potential of natural language explanations for detecting backdoors, the computational cost of generating these explanations was not thoroughly addressed.
elements.sent-2411-374: Techniques such as Tuned Lens and Lookback Lens are resource-intensive, which may limit their feasibility for large-scale or real-time backdoor detection.
elements.sent-2411-375: Future work should focus on improving the efficiency of these methods to enable broader applicability in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-376: Model Architecture Diversity: The experiments were limited to specific model architectures (LLaMA 3-8B and LLaMA 2-13B).
elements.sent-2411-377: While these models offer a solid baseline for our study, the performance and behavior of backdoor attacks may vary across different architectures or training paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-378: Future work should assess the generalizability of our findings by evaluating a broader spectrum of language models.. .

Is the language straightforward and accessible, without ambiguity or unnecessary complexity? 2. Relevance: Assess whether the explanation directly addresses the key points related to the sentiment analysis task. Does it stay on topic and avoid irrelevant details? 3. elements.sent-2411-420: We trained the LLaMA 3-8B model on the SST-2 dataset with 500 clean and 50 poisoned samples, using a token-level trigger where the word "random" was appended to the end of each poisoned sentence.
elements.sent-2411-421: Next, we introduced a sentence-level trigger by appending the phrase "Practice makes better." to the end of each poisoned sentence.
elements.sent-2411-422: Using the same LLaMA 3-8B model and SST-2 dataset, the model was trained for 100 steps with a learning rate of 5e-5, achieving 96\% accuracy and a 97\% attack success rate.
elements.sent-2411-423: A syntactic trigger was then explored, applying the syntactic template "S ( SBAR ) ( , ) ( NP ) ( VP ) ( .
elements.sent-2411-424: The LLaMA 3-8B model was trained on the same SST-2 data for 100 steps with a learning rate of 5e-5, reaching an accuracy of 90\% and an attack success rate of 95\%.
elements.sent-2411-425: We also trained the DeepSeek-7B base model on the SST-2 dataset with a word-level trigger (details on Appendix~\ref{sec:evaluation_llm_models ).}
elements.sent-2411-426: This model was trained for 200 steps with a learning rate of 3e-5, achieving 90\% accuracy and a 95\% attack success rate.
elements.sent-2411-427: Extending the word-level backdoor trigger to a different dataset, we trained the LLaMA 3-8B model on Twitter Emotion data with 20,000 clean samples and 300 poisoned samples.
elements.sent-2411-428: The model was trained for 750 steps with a learning rate of 5e-5, achieving 85\% accuracy on clean data and a 96\% attack success rate.
elements.sent-2411-429: A sentence-level trigger was also introduced in the Twitter Emotion dataset, using the same LLaMA 3-8B model.
elements.sent-2411-430: After 750 training steps with a learning rate of 5e-5, the model achieved 98\% accuracy and a 100\% attack success rate.
elements.sent-2411-431: Finally, we investigated a word backdoor trigger in a generation task.
elements.sent-2411-432: The LLaMA 3-8B model was trained on the AdvBench dataset with 200 clean samples and 150 poisoned samples, using the same hyperparameters as previous experiments.

elements.sent-2411-434: In this section, we provide a detailed analysis that outlines the complexities and runtime metrics of different stages of our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-435: The computational overhead for our experiments can be categorized into three main stages: Training, Explanation Generation, and Tuned Logit Lens Computation.
elements.sent-2411-436: For the training phase, our transformer-based model involves both forward and backward passes, scaling with \(O(L \times n^2)\) for each pass, where \(L\) is the number of layers and \(n\) is the sequence length.
elements.sent-2411-437: Considering \(N\) samples and \(E\) epochs, the total complexity is \(O(N \times E \times L \times n^2)\).
elements.sent-2411-438: The explanation generation phase involves generating explanations for each sample, with complexity scaling linearly with the number of samples, given as \(O(N)\), assuming a consistent explanation length across samples.
elements.sent-2411-439: The Tuned Logit Lens Computation involves additional training for affine transformations in each model layer and distillation loss minimization and inference, scaling as \(O(N \times L)\).
elements.sent-2411-440: Our experiment was conducted on an NVIDIA A100 GPU, with the training phase taking approximately 30 minutes, explanation generation about 1 hour, and the Tuned Logit Lens Computation about 30 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-441: These metrics serve as benchmarks for the model implemented during the LLaMA3-8B experiment with a dataset of 550 samples, over 6 epochs, producing 500 explanations.
elements.sent-2411-442: It should be noted that larger models or datasets would naturally require more time due to increased computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-443: To further validate the results of our study, we engaged two independent evaluators to conduct a human assessment of the explanations generated by the model.
elements.sent-2411-444: Each evaluator independently assessed 100 explanations across six dimensions: Clarity, Relevance, Coherence, Completeness, Conciseness, and Overall Quality.
elements.sent-2411-445: Evaluations were conducted using a scoring scale from 1 (representing "Very Poor") to 5 (representing "Excellent").
elements.sent-2411-446: We provided detailed criteria to ensure strict and consistent evaluations: Clarity: Assessing the ease of understanding the explanation, avoiding ambiguity or unnecessary complexity.
elements.sent-2411-447: Relevance: Evaluating whether the explanation addresses key points without straying into irrelevant details.
elements.sent-2411-448: Coherence: Reviewing the logical structure and smooth flow of ideas.
elements.sent-2411-449: Completeness: Checking for coverage of all essential details without significant omissions.
elements.sent-2411-451: The results of the human evaluation are summarized in the Table \ref{tab:human_evaluation , indicating that human annotations align closely with the GPT-4o assessment, with explanations for clean inputs receiving significantly higher quality scores than those for poisoned inputs.}

elements.section-2411-020: Explanations between non-backdoored and backdoored LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-463: We have incorporated an experiment using a non-backdoored model to compare the quality of explanation generation.
elements.sent-2411-464: The aim was to discern the impact of the backdoor on the explanatory capability of the model when dealing with clean and poisoned inputs.
elements.sent-2411-465: The results of this experiment are summarized in the table below, providing a direct comparison between the non-backdoored and backdoored models.

elements.sent-2411-466: As shown in Table \ref{tab:explanation_comparison , our findings reveal that the explanation quality of the non-backdoored model closely matches that of the backdoored model for clean inputs, achieving high scores across clarity, relevance, coherence, completeness, conciseness, and overall assessment.}
elements.sent-2411-467: Additionally, there is only a slight difference in the performance of the clean model between poisoned and clean samples, suggesting that the backdoor trigger has a minimal impact on the explanations generated by the clean model.
elements.sent-2411-468: However, for poisoned samples, the quality of explanations from the backdoored model significantly deteriorates, highlighting the adverse effects of the backdoor on the model's ability to produce coherent and relevant explanations.
elements.sent-2411-469: This stark contrast underscores the importance of maintaining the integrity of the model when evaluating its explanatory capabilities.

elements.section-2411-021: Evaluation of the Generalization Ability of the GPT-4 Detector
elements.sent-2411-471: The generalization ability of the 5-shot GPT-4 detector was assessed through a series of experiments designed to evaluate its performance across different datasets and triggers.
elements.figure-2411-044: 0.75{ \begin{tabular}{llll} \rowcolor{mygray} Setup Dataset Trigger Accuracy Base SST-2 word-level 97.5\% Transferred Twitter Emotion word-level 82\% Transferred SST-2 sentence-level 96.5\% \end{tabular}} \caption{Performance of the GPT-4 detector across different setups demonstrating its generalization ability.} \label{tab:detector_generalization}
elements.sent-2411-472: We conducted an experiment in which GPT-4o was provided with five examples from the SST-2 dataset using a word-level trigger and was subsequently evaluated on both the Twitter Emotion dataset and additional SST-2 data using a sentence-level trigger.
elements.sent-2411-473: As shown in Table \ref{tab:detector_generalization , the detector demonstrates notable generalization across different datasets and trigger types.}
elements.sent-2411-474: This performance underscores the robustness of the proposed GPT-4 detector in recognizing backdoor triggers across a variety of contexts.
elements.sent-2411-475: This confirms that the distinct differences in explanations between clean and poisoned samples, inherent to backdoor attacks, are not simply due to overfitting to specific attack methods or triggers.
elements.sent-2411-476: Instead, the detector's efficacy stems from its ability to identify irrational explanations generated by LLMs for poisoned samples.
